concerns.text: Abstract claims 'improves long-document VQA scores by 7.1%' (line 24), but Table 1 (line 108) shows an absolute improvement of 7.11 points (57.70 - 50.59). The text uses '%' to denote absolute percentage points, which is logically ambiguous and contradicts the standard interpretation of relative improvement. The conclusion does not strictly follow the precise data presentation without clarifying 'percentage points'.

Section 6.2 (line 234) claims the model 'maintains strong performance at 256K and 512K... exceeding baselines by over 20%'. Table 2 (line 242) shows model score 53.80 vs baseline 28.80. The relative improvement is (53.80-28.80)/28.80 ≈ 86.8%. The claim 'exceeding baselines by over 20%' is logically true but severely understates the result, potentially misleading the reader about the magnitude of the gain. The conclusion is supported but the phrasing is inconsistent with the data's implication of a much larger margin.

Section 5.3 (line 182) concludes 'pure long-context training does not severely degrade short-context capabilities' based on a drop from 66.47 to 65.48 (1.5% relative drop). However, Table 3 (line 176) shows specific benchmarks like MMMU dropping from 53.00 to 49.33 (7% drop) and MMBench from 80.68 to 80.00. The generalization from 'mild drop' to 'not severely degrade' is logically sound for the aggregate, but the specific benchmark drops (MMMU) are non-trivial. The conclusion holds for the aggregate metric but lacks nuance regarding specific capability degradation.
